title: Yank and Put
subtitle: Yank is copy. Put is paste. The register knows whether it's a line or a span.
summary: y is the yank (copy) operator. p puts after the cursor; P puts before. The register remembers whether it holds characters or whole lines, and that decides where the put lands.

blocks.text: {key:y} is *yank* — Vim's word for copy. It's an operator, like {key:d} and {key:c}, so it takes a motion: {key:y}{key:w} yanks a word, {key:y}{key:$} yanks to end of line, {key:y}{key:y} yanks the whole line.
Whatever you yanked sits in the unnamed register, ready to be put with {key:p} (after the cursor) or {key:P} (before).

blocks.label: Yank current line (= yy by tradition; some configs map to y$)

blocks.title: Why p Sometimes Goes Below the Line and Sometimes After the Cursor
blocks.text:
Every register holds **(text, type)** where `type` is `char`, `line`, or `block`. The type is set by *how the text got into the register*:

  • {key:y}{key:w}, {key:y}{key:$}, {key:y}{key:i}{key:w} → type = `char` (a span of characters).
  • {key:y}{key:y}, {key:y}{key:j}, {key:y}{key:G} → type = `line` (one or more whole lines).
  • {key:Ctrl-V}-then-{key:y} → type = `block` (a rectangle).

When you put:

  • A `char`-typed register goes *after the cursor* (just past the current character).
  • A `line`-typed register goes *on the next line below*.
  • A `block`-typed register goes *as a rectangle starting one column right of the cursor*.

This is why {key:y}{key:y}{key:p} "duplicates the line" (the line-typed register lands as a new line below) and {key:y}{key:w}{key:p} "appends a word after the cursor." It's not magic; it's the type tag.

{key:P} (capital) does the mirror: charwise = before cursor; linewise = above current line; blockwise = at the cursor column (no shift).

blocks.title: The Y Wart
blocks.text:
By symmetry with {key:D} = {key:d}{key:$} and {key:C} = {key:c}{key:$}, you'd expect {key:Y} = {key:y}{key:$}. It isn't.

In classic Vim, {key:Y} is just another way to say {key:y}{key:y}. This is a deliberate compatibility choice — `vi` behaved that way, so Vim did too. It's the most common single complaint about Vim's grammar.

Neovim 0.6+ changed the default: in Neovim, {key:Y} is now {key:y}{key:$} (matching {key:D} and {key:C}). In Vim you can opt in with `:nnoremap Y y$`. The video lessons show classic Y; Neovim users get the new behavior for free.
Move text without using a separate buffer: {key:d}{key:d} (cut a line), navigate, {key:p} (paste below). It's exactly the cut-and-paste you already know, just expressed in operator+motion.

rows: Yank line (`vi`) or to end-of-line (nvim default)
Like p but leave cursor *after* pasted text

blocks.title: Yank produces a typed snapshot, not raw text
blocks.text: A yank captures both payload and type (`char`, `line`, or `block`). Paste reads that type tag and chooses placement: charwise pastes after the cursor on the same line; linewise pastes on a new line below; blockwise pastes as a rectangle. The register is a tiny tagged-union struct, not a string. Mismatch the type and {key:p} appears to misbehave — it's just honoring the tag you stored.
